Latest 10-K business section (newest first):
  filing_date=2022-03-11 section=business period_end=2022-01-31
ITEM 1.    BUSINESS
Overview
Salesforce is a global leader in customer relationship management ("CRM") technology that brings companies and their customers together in the digital age. Founded in 1999, we enable companies of every size and industry to take advantage of powerful technologies, including cloud, mobile, social, blockchain and artificial intelligence, to connect to their customers in a whole new way and help them transform their businesses around the customer in this digital-first world.
With our Customer 360 platform, we deliver a single source of truth, connecting customer data across systems, apps and devices to help companies with their digital transformation. Customer 360 gives teams sales, service, marketing and commerce capabilities and more, and a single shared view of their customers so they can work together to build lasting, trusted relationships and deliver the personalized experiences their customers expect. With our acquisition of Slack Technologies, Inc. ("Slack") in July 2021, we are also creating a new digital headquarters where companies, employees, governments, and stakeholders can create success from anywhere.
Our Customer 360 service offerings are designed to be flexible, scalable and easy to use. They can generally be configured easily, rapidly deployed and integrated with other platforms and enterprise applications. We sell to businesses worldwide, primarily on a subscription basis, through our direct sales efforts and also indirectly through partners. We also enable third parties to use our platform and developer tools to create additional functionality and new applications that run on our platform, which are sold separately from, or in conjunction with, our service offerings.
Salesforce is committed to a core set of values: trust, customer success, innovation, equality and sustainability. Foremost among these is trust, which is the foundation for everything we do. Our customers trust our technology to deliver the highest levels of security, privacy, performance, compliance and availability at scale. Customer success is at the core of our business and we align the entire company around our customers' needs to ensure their success and prove our value. We believe in continuous innovation, enabling our customers to access the latest technology advances so they can innovate, build and stay ahead in their industries. Equality is a core tenet of how we run our business. We value the equality of every individual at our company and in our community. We believe that creating a diverse workplace that reflects the communities we serve and fostering an inclusive culture where everyone feels seen, heard and valued makes us a better company. Finally, we believe the world is in a climate crisis and that sustainability, including bold climate action, is the only way forward. We're bringing the full power of Salesforce to help organizations achieve net zero.
We believe that our values create value, and the business of business is to make the world a better place for all of our stakeholders, including stockholders, customers, employees, partners, the planet and the communities in which we work and live. Salesforce is committed to giving back to our communities, closing the inequality gap and helping businesses grow while protecting the environment for future generations. We believe we have a broad responsibili
…[truncated 18628 chars]…
s that run on the customers' current infrastructure or as hosted services.
We believe more companies may become competitive threats due to the accelerated shift to cloud and hosted service offerings and customer experience management solutions. We also expect our competition to change and evolve as we expand into more markets, with new offerings and verticals.
Customers
We sell to businesses of all sizes and in almost every industry worldwide. The number of paying subscriptions at each of our customers ranges from one to hundreds of thousands. None of our customers accounted for more than five percent of our revenues in fiscal years 2022, 2021 or 2020. In addition, we do not have any material dependencies on any specific product, service or particular group or groups.
Customer Service and Support
We offer professional services to help customers achieve business results faster with Salesforce solutions. Our architects and innovation program teams act as advisors to plan and execute digital transformations for our customers. This includes implementation services for multi-cloud and complex deployments. We provide best-practices and AI-based recommendations and adoption programs globally. In addition, we provide advanced education, including in-person and online courses to certify our customers and partners on architecting, administering, deploying and developing our service offerings.
Our global customer support group responds to both business and technical inquiries about the use of our products via the web, telephone, email, social networks and other channels. We provide standard customer support during regular business hours at no charge to customers who purchase any of our paying subscription editions. We also offer premier customer support that is either included in a premium success offering or sold for an additional fee, which can include services such as priority access to technical resources, developer support and system administration. In addition, we offer a premier priority support add-on that is designed to provide customers technical account management with responses for incidents from a dedicated team knowledgeable about the customer's specific enterprise architecture, which offers proactive monitoring and instruction to optimize their usage of our products.
Sales and Marketing
We sell our services primarily through our direct sales force, which comprises of telephone sales personnel based in regional hubs, field sales personnel based in territories close to their customers and self service offerings. Both our telephone sales and field sales personnel are supported by sales representatives who are primarily responsible for generating qualified sales leads.
To a lesser extent, we also utilize a network of partners who refer sales leads to us and assist in selling to these prospects. This network includes global consulting firms, systems integrators and other partners. In return, we typically pay these partners a fee based on the first-year subscription revenue generated by the customers whom they refer. We continue to invest in developing additional distribution channels for our subscription services.
We use a variety of marketing programs across traditional and social channels to target our prospective and current customers, partners and developers. We focus our marketing activities on the cities and countries with the largest market opportunities. Our primary marketing activities include:
- multichannel marketing campaigns that span email, social media, the web, television and more, which align to a broader customer journey;
- in-person and virtual customer events of all sizes to create customer and prospect awareness, including proprietary events such as Dreamforce and our virtual Dreamforce to You, World Tours, and other virtual events, as well as participation in trade shows and industry events;
- live events and original programming on Salesforce+, our streaming platform which launched in fiscal 2022, which includes discussions about the future of technology in the digital-first, work anywhere world and educational content to learn new skills and pursue new career opportunities;
- press and industry analyst relations to garner third-party validation and generate positive coverage for our company, brand, service offerings and value proposition;
- partner co-marketing activities with global and regional implementation partners;
- customer testimonials and our community of Trailblazers: individuals who drive innovation, grow their careers and transform their businesses using the Customer 360 platform;
- in-person and virtual technology event sponsorships;
- event partnerships with high-profile global brands and organizations; and
8
Table of Contents
- primary real estate signage.
Intellectual Property
We rely on a combination of trademarks, copyrights, trade secrets and patents, as well as contractual provisions, to protect our proprietary technology and our brands. We also enter into confidentiality and proprietary rights agreements with our employees, consultants and other third parties and control access to software, services, documentation and other proprietary information. We believe the duration of our patents is adequate relative to the expected lives of our service offerings. We also purchase or license technology that we incorporate into our products or services. At times, we make select intellectual property broadly available at no or low cost to achieve a strategic objective, such as promoting industry standards, advancing interoperability, fostering open source software or attracting and enabling our external development community. While it may be necessary in the future to seek or renew licenses relating to various aspects of our products and business methods, we believe, based upon past experience and industry practice, such licenses generally could be obtained on commercially reasonable terms.
Human Capital Management
Salesforce is committed to a core set of values: trust, customer success, innovation, equality and sustainability. These core values are the foundation of our company culture, which we believe is fundamental to, and a competitive advantage in, our approach to managing our workforce. We believe our company culture fosters open dialogue, collaboration, recognition and a sense of family, all of which allow us to attract and retain the best talent, which is critical for our continued success. For example, our sales, engineering and customer success teams are critical to our ability to grow, innovate and ensure the trust and customer success of our customers.
We believe our efforts in managing our workforce have been effective. Our focus on our workplace environment and a strong company culture has led to recognition across the globe, as evidenced by the following awards: Fortune World's Most Admired Companies (2022 and for the eighth year in a row), Fortune 100 Best Companies to Work (2021 and for the 13th year in a row), Human Rights Campaign Best Places to Work For LGBTQ Equality (2022), and Glassdoor Employees' Choice Best Place to Work in Canada, France, Germany, the United Kingdom, and the United States (2022).
As of January 31, 2022, we had 73,541 employees, of which approximately 55 percent were located in the United States and 45 percent were located internationally and approximately 36 percent identified as women, 64 percent identified as men and less than 1 percent identified as non-binary. None of our employees in the United States are represented by a labor union. However, employees of certain foreign subsidiaries are represented by works councils.
Our ability to attract and retain the best talent is even more important as our employees adapt to a new normal with how and where we work. Maintaining a strong company culture, in a remote environment, and at a time of rapid growth in our workforce remains a priority throughout our human capital management p

CRM.quantitative_report:
**Quantitative Analysis: CRM (as of 2022-07-01)**

**1. Financial Trajectory**
*   **Revenue:** Consistent quarterly growth from $5.15B (Q2 2020) to $7.41B (Q1 2022). Latest YoY growth is +24.3%, but QoQ growth slowed to +1.2%.
*   **Earnings & Margins:** EPS trajectory is sharply negative. EPS was $2.85 in Q2 2020 and $0.03 in Q1 2022, a -94.0% YoY decline. Net margin compressed from 0.199 (Jul 2021) to 0.036 (Jun 2022). Operating margin collapsed to 0.008 in Q1 2022 from 0.056 in Q2 2021.
*   **Cash Flow:** Data unavailable for the entire lookback period.
*   **Balance Sheet:** Total assets grew from $57.78B (Q2 2020) to $93.02B (Q1 2022). Equity grew from $38.44B to $58.88B. Total debt increased from $2.69B to $10.60B.

**2. Earnings Quality & Balance-Sheet Resilience**
*   **Earnings Quality:** Deteriorating. TTM net income is $1.00B on $27.94B revenue, yielding a low 3.6% net margin. The latest quarter's operating income of $20.00M represents an operating margin of 0.008 (0.8%), indicating minimal profit after costs.
*   **Balance-Sheet Resiliness:** Moderate. Debt-to-equity is 0.18. The equity base ($58.88B) supports assets, but liabilities grew from $19.34B to $34.15B over the period.

**3. Valuation vs. History**
*   Current valuation multiples are at the high end of their recent ranges and are not supported by current earnings power.
*   **P/E Diluted:** 164.7, within a 365-day range of 50.3 to 188.8.
*   **P/B:** 2.806, within a range of 2.6 to 5.9.
*   **P/S:** 5.913.
*   **Earnings Yield:** 0.006 (0.6%).
*   **ROE:** 0.017 (1.7%), down from 0.104 (10.4%) in Jul 2021.

**4. Price Context vs. Fundamentals**
*   The price has declined significantly, moving from $254.54 (Dec 2021) to $165.04 (Jun 2022), near its 365-day low of $154.55.
*   **Quarterly Returns:** Q2 2022: -23.2%; Q1 2022: -15.6%; Q4 2021: -5.9%.
*   The price decline aligns with the deterioration in earnings and margins, though the valuation multiples (P/E, P/S) remain elevated relative to the diminished profitability.

**5. Strongest Quantitative Upside & Downside**
*   **Upside:** Continued top-line growth (+24.3% YoY) and a high gross margin (73.1%) provide a foundation for profitability recovery if operating expenses are controlled. The balance sheet is not over-leveraged (Debt/Equity: 0.18).
*   **Downside:** Sustained margin compression, with operating margin at 0.008. Earnings are near zero, making the high valuation (P/E: 164.7) vulnerable to a further de-rating. The lack of cash flow data is a critical information gap.

**6. Provisional Quantitative Risk/Reward Conclusion**
The quantitative evidence presents a challenging picture. Strong revenue growth is offset by a collapse in profitability and elevated valuation multiples. The stock's price decline has been severe, reflecting this deterioration, but key valuation metrics (P/E 164.7, P/S 5.9) remain high relative to the company's current negligible earnings yield (0.6%). Balance sheet strength provides a cushion, but without visibility into cash flow generation or a clear inflection point in operating margins, the quantitative risk/reward profile appears unfavorable. The primary risk is further valuation contraction; the primary upside is a fundamental reversion in margin trends not yet visible in the data.
CRM.qualitative_report:
**Qualitative Analysis: Salesforce (CRM)**
**Decision Date: 2022-07-01 (Evidence strictly prior)**

**1. Business/Franchise Read (from 10-K, period ending 2022-01-31)**
Salesforce is the global leader in Customer Relationship Management (CRM) software. Its core value proposition is the "Customer 360" platform, which provides a unified view of customer data across sales, service, marketing, and commerce. The business model is primarily subscription-based, serving companies of all sizes and industries globally. A key strategic move was the July 2021 acquisition of Slack, aimed at creating a "digital headquarters" for employee and customer collaboration, which is being integrated into the platform. Growth strategy is centered on cross-selling/up-selling to the existing base, global expansion, entering new categories and verticals, and fostering a large partner ecosystem (ISVs, system integrators). The technology is delivered as a scalable, multi-tenant cloud service, with ongoing investment in Hyperforce to enable deployment on major public clouds.

**2. Recent Developments (Dated from supplied text)**
*   **2022-05-31:** Salesforce reported earnings and raised its full-year adjusted profit forecast. Management stated it did not see any "material impact" from the broader economic environment, citing resilient demand for business software. The stock subsequently rose.
*   **2022-06-01:** Analysis and articles highlighted that the company's "resilient demand" and "great quarter" were seen as positive signals amid a tech downturn, supporting the stock's rise.
*   **2022-06-28:** The stock was noted as a trending subject, but without new material company-specific news beyond the prior earnings beat and guidance raise.
*   **2022-06-30:** General market news noted the S&P 500 and Nasdaq were experiencing significant downturns, cementing a historically poor first half of the year. An article noted CRM had fallen 3.1% since its last earnings report (from 2022-05-31), framing it as a potential rebound candidate.

**3. Material Qualitative Risks and Offsets**
*   **Risk: Intense and Evolving Competition.** The 10-K (2022-03-11) states the market is "highly competitive, rapidly evolving and fragmented... with low barriers to entry." Competitors include traditional software vendors, providers of free products, niche specialists, and large platform companies. The company's expansion into new markets (analytics, commerce, integration) increases exposure to established players in those spaces (e.g., Snowflake, noted in 2022-06-28 news as executing strongly).
*   **Risk: Macroeconomic & Market Sentiment.** As of 2022-06-30, the broader equity market was in a significant downturn, which can pressure software multiples regardless of company-specific fundamentals. While Salesforce guided through this (2022-05-31), a protracted downturn could eventually affect customer spending.
*   **Risk: Execution of Large-Scale Integrations.** The strategy is reliant on successfully integrating major acquisitions like Slack (2021), Tableau, and MuleSoft to deliver on the "Customer 360" vision.
*   **Offset: Demonstrated Demand Resilience.** The updated profit forecast and management commentary (2022-05-31) indicate that demand for digital transformation and CRM software remains strong despite macroeconomic headwinds.
*   **Offset: Large, Diversified Customer Base & Recurring Revenue.** No single customer accounts for more than 5% of revenue (10-K, 2022-03-11). The subscription model provides revenue visibility.
*   **Offset: Strategic Tailwinds.** The narrative cites ongoing "digital transformation" and the shift to "digital-first" and "work-from-anywhere" as persistent drivers for the company's core offerings and new categories like collaboration (Slack).

**4. Qualitative Assessment: Neutral**
The fundamental business narrative remains strong: Salesforce is the clear market leader in a growing CRM market with a broad product suite and clear expansion vectors. The recent earnings report (2022-05-31) provided tangible evidence of resilient demand, leading to a positive guidance revision. However, this positive operational update is counterbalanced by significant headwinds. The competitive landscape is acknowledged as fierce and evolving. Most pressingly, the macro and equity market environment as of the decision date (2022-06-30) is negative and may overshadow near-term company-specific performance. The stock's weakness post-earnings despite the raised guidance reflects this conflict. The assessment is **Neutral**, weighing resilient business execution against a challenging competitive and market backdrop.

corrections.issue: Quant report states 'Net margin compressed from 0.199 (Jul 2021) to 0.036 (Jun 2022)' — the 0.199 figure corresponds to the 2021-07-02 valuation snapshot, which uses TTM net income. The raw income statement shows Q2 2021 (2021-07-31) net income of $535M on $6.34B revenue (8.4% quarterly margin), not 19.9%. The 19.9% TTM net margin in Jul 2021 was inflated by the Q2 2020 quarter containing a large $2.62B net income figure (likely a one-time gain). The quant report does not flag this distortion, which materially affects the margin-compression narrative.
corrections.required_action: Add a note that the Jul 2021 TTM net margin of 19.9% was elevated by a one-time or non-recurring item in Q2 2020 ($2.62B net income vs ~$178M operating income), making the margin compression appear steeper than the underlying operating trend. The operating-margin trajectory (0.056 → 0.008) is a cleaner measure of deterioration.

corrections.issue: Quant report states 'EPS was $2.85 in Q2 2020' and uses this as the starting point for the -94% YoY decline. The raw data confirms Q2 2020 EPS of $2.85, but the income statement shows operating income of only $178M that quarter — the $2.62B net income was driven by non-operating items. The report does not flag this, which overstates the severity of the earnings decline from an operational standpoint.
corrections.required_action: Note that the Q2 2020 EPS of $2.85 was driven by a large non-operating gain (net income $2.62B vs operating income $178M), so the YoY EPS comparison is distorted. The TTM operating income decline from ~$1.09B (trailing through Q2 2020) to $214M (trailing through Q1 2022) better captures the operational deterioration.

corrections.issue: Qual report states 'The stock subsequently rose' after the 2022-05-31 earnings report. The raw news confirms Salesforce 'rose the most in almost two years' on the earnings beat. However, the qual report also notes the stock fell 3.1% since the earnings report (as of 2022-06-30). The report frames the post-earnings weakness as reflecting macro headwinds, but does not reconcile this with the quant data showing continued margin deterioration in Q1 2022 (operating margin 0.008) — the fundamental deterioration may be contributing to the post-earnings fade, not just macro sentiment.
corrections.required_action: Acknowledge that the post-earnings stock decline may reflect not only macro headwinds but also the very low Q1 2022 operating margin (0.8%) reported in the same earnings cycle, which the qual report does not cross-reference.

unsupported_claims: : The quant report's statement 'Net margin compressed from 0.199 (Jul 2021) to 0.036 (Jun 2022)' is numerically consistent with the raw valuation data but misleadingly presents a TTM figure inflated by a one-time Q2 2020 gain as if it were a normal baseline margin. The raw income statement shows operating margins in the 3-6% range throughout 2021, not ~20%.

: The quant report's claim of 'Operating margin collapsed to 0.008 in Q1 2022 from 0.056 in Q2 2021' is supported by the raw data (Q1 2022 operating income $20M / $7.41B ≈ 0.27%, and Q2 2021 operating income $332M / $6.34B ≈ 5.2%). The 0.008 figure is actually understated — $20M/$7.41B = 0.0027, not 0.008. However, the raw valuation pack confirms operating_margin=0.008, so this may reflect a different calculation methodology (e.g., TTM). The quant report should clarify whether 0.008 is quarterly or TTM.

pm_decision_obj.executive_summary: Salesforce remains the dominant CRM platform with resilient demand and strong revenue growth (+24.3% YoY), but a near-zero operating margin (0.8% in Q1 2022) and elevated valuation (P/E 164.7) create a risk/reward that is roughly balanced. The stock's significant price decline has brought it near 365-day lows, partially reflecting the deterioration, but the valuation still prices in substantial margin recovery that is not yet visible in the data.
pm_decision_obj.investment_thesis: Salesforce remains the dominant CRM platform with resilient demand and strong revenue growth (+24.3% YoY), but a near-zero operating margin (0.8% in Q1 2022) and elevated valuation (P/E 164.7) create a risk/reward that is roughly balanced. The stock's significant price decline has brought it near 365-day lows, partially reflecting the deterioration, but the valuation still prices in substantial margin recovery that is not yet visible in the data.
key_factors: Revenue growth of +24.3% YoY to $7.41B in Q1 2022 demonstrates continued demand resilience, with management raising full-year profit guidance on 2022-05-31 citing no 'material impact' from the macro environment.

Operating margin collapsed to approximately 0.8% in Q1 2022 ($20M operating income on $7.41B revenue), down from ~5.2% in Q2 2021, reflecting heavy investment in Slack integration and sales capacity that has not yet translated to profitability.

Valuation remains elevated at P/E 164.7 and P/S 5.9 despite the price decline to $165.04 (near 365-day low of $154.55), with an earnings yield of only 0.6% — the market is pricing in a margin recovery that the data does not yet confirm.

Balance sheet provides a cushion with debt-to-equity of 0.18 and $58.88B equity, but total debt has quadrupled from $2.69B to $10.60B since Q2 2020, and the lack of cash flow data is a critical information gap.

IBM.quantitative_report:
**IBM Quantitative Report (Decision Date: 2022-07-01)**  

**1. Revenue, Earnings, Margin, Cash-Flow, and Balance-Sheet Trajectory**  
- **Revenue:** Quarterly revenue declined YoY by -19.9% (Q1 2022: $14.20B vs Q1 2021: $17.73B). The QoQ jump of +335.9% from Q4 2021 ($3.26B) is distorted by the Kyndryl spin-off, reducing comparability. TTM revenue is $53.82B.  
- **Earnings:** Q1 2022 EPS diluted of $0.81 is down -23.6% YoY. The TTM net income is $5.52B.  
- **Margins:** Supplied trailing gross margin is 56.9%, operating margin 8.5%, net margin 10.3%. The Q1 2022 quarterly gross margin computed from financials is 51.6% ($7.33B / $14.20B).  
- **Cash-Flow:** No cash-flow data supplied; free cash flow is unavailable.  
- **Balance Sheet:** Total assets declined from $155.97B (2020-12-31) to $133.28B (2022-03-31). Total equity is stable near $19.11B, while total liabilities are $114.16B, yielding a debt-to-equity ratio of 5.97x.  

**2. Earnings Quality and Balance-Sheet Resilience**  
- **Earnings Quality:** Earnings volatility is high due to spin-off effects (e.g., Q4 2021 EPS of $2.58 vs Q1 2022 of $0.81). YoY EPS decline of -23.6% signals weakening core profitability. The high gross margin (56.9%) contrasts with a low operating margin (8.5%), indicating significant non-operating or overhead costs.  
- **Balance-Sheet Resilience:** Equity is thin ($19.11B) relative to liabilities ($114.16B), resulting in a debt-to-equity ratio of 5.97x. The declining asset base may reflect post-spin-off restructuring but increases leverage risk.  

**3. Valuation Versus Supplied History**  
- **P/E Diluted:** Latest is 23.251, within the 365-day range of 19.1 to 26.3.  
- **P/B:** Latest is 6.717, at the top of the 365-day range of 4.7 to 6.8.  
- **Earnings Yield:** 4.3% (inverse of P/E).  
- **ROE:** 28.9%, elevated relative to historical values (range 0.2–0.3), partly due to high leverage.  
- **Context:** Valuation metrics are mid-to-high historical ranges despite recent fundamental weakness.  

**4. Price Level and Quarterly/Annual Return Context**  
- **Price Level:** $141.19 (2022-06-30) is near the 52-week high of $144.73 (range low $114.56).  
- **Quarterly Returns:** +6.9% over the latest quarter (from $132.13 on 2022-03-30). Prior two quarters were flat (-0.9% and +0.2%).  
- **Fundamental Alignment:** Price momentum (+6.9% quarterly) contrasts with YoY revenue (-19.9%) and earnings (-23.6%) declines, indicating disconnect.  

**5. Strongest Quantitative Upside and Downside**  
- **Upside:** Gross margin (56.9%) remains high, suggesting pricing power or mix shift. P/E (23.25) is not at the historical high (26.3), leaving room if earnings stabilize. Recent price trend is positive.  
- **Downside:** High leverage (debt-to-equity 5.97x) amplifies earnings risk. YoY revenue and earnings declines may persist. Price near 52-week high limits upside; P/B at historical peak (6.717) implies premium valuation for declining returns.  

**6. Provisional Quantitative Risk/Reward Conclusion**  
The quantitative profile shows deteriorating fundamentals (YoY revenue -19.9%, EPS -23.6%) coupled with high leverage (debt-to-equity 5.97x). Valuation is not cheap relative to history (P/E mid-range, P/B peak) despite weak growth. Price momentum (+6.9% quarterly) is incongruent with underlying trends, suggesting risk of mean reversion. Cash-flow data absence limits full assessment, but the balance sheet’s high liability-to-equity ratio and declining earnings trajectory indicate skewed risk/reward to the downside. Provisional conclusion: IBM is quantitatively unattractive given the fundamental-path divergence and balance-sheet strain.
IBM.qualitative_report:
**Qualitative Report: IBM**  
**Decision Date:** 2022-07-01  

---

### 1. Business/Franchise Read from Narrative
IBM is described as a legacy technology company undergoing a strategic shift toward hybrid cloud and artificial intelligence. The Red Hat acquisition (2019) remains central to this transition, with evidence noting its integration into partner ecosystems (e.g., Hewlett Packard Enterprise’s GreenLake, dated 2022-06-28). IBM’s franchise is framed around hybrid cloud infrastructure, software, and consulting, with strategic imperatives cited as growing drivers of organic growth at constant currency (SeekingAlpha, 2022-06-10).

---

### 2. Recent Developments (Dated Evidence)
- **Legal Liability:** IBM was ordered to pay BMC Software **$1.6 billion** in a lawsuit related to poaching the AT&T account (2022-05-31, 2022-06-01). The verdict was linked to contractual breaches and business practices.
- **Settlement:** IBM settled an age discrimination lawsuit shortly after being ordered to produce internal emails from former executives discussing reductions in older workers (2022-06-29).
- **Market Perception:** Commentary suggests IBM stock may face pressure relative to high-growth tech peers, with one article favoring Datadog over IBM due to growth prospects (2022-06-29). IBM is cited as a dividend stock with yields around 4.3–4.4% at certain price points (2022-06-27).
- **Industry Context:** Stalled U.S. semiconductor funding legislation ($52 billion) could affect tech sector dynamics, though IBM’s direct exposure is not specified (2022-06-29).

---

### 3. Material Qualitative Risks and Offsets
**Risks:**  
- **Legal/Financial Liabilities:** The $1.6B BMC verdict and age discrimination settlement present material financial and reputational risks, highlighting alleged contractual and HR misconduct (2022-05-31, 2022-06-29).  
- **Legacy Business Decline:** Repeated narrative references to IBM as a “beaten-down” stock suggest market skepticism about its ability to compete with cloud-native rivals (2022-06-29, 2022-06-30).  
- **Punitive Damages Precedent:** A judge noted IBM’s “routine eschewal of rules” in awarding punitive damages, indicating potential for future legal challenges (2022-05-31).  

**Offsets:**  
- **Strategic Redeployment:** The hybrid cloud/AI pivot, anchored by Red Hat, is cited as a growth catalyst in multiple markets (SeekingAlpha, 2022-06-10).  
- **Dividend Appeal:** IBM’s dividend yield (4.3–4.4%) may attract income-focused investors during market volatility (2022-06-27).  
- **Partner Ecosystem Expansion:** Red Hat’s adoption by third parties (e.g., HPE) suggests continued relevance in enterprise infrastructure (2022-06-28).  

---

### 4. Concise Qualitative Assessment
**Neutral to Slightly Negative.**  
The evidence confirms IBM is executing a strategic shift toward hybrid cloud, supported by Red Hat’s integration and growing partner adoption. However, recent legal developments—particularly the $1.6B verdict and age discrimination case—introduce material financial, operational, and reputational risks. Market sentiment remains skeptical of IBM’s growth relative to peers, offset only by its dividend appeal. The lack of recent operational guidance in the supplied text limits visibility into near-term execution.

---  
**Note:** All assessments derive solely from the provided point-in-time evidence. Financial figures are only referenced where directly stated in the source text.

corrections.issue: Quant report states 'ROE: 28.9%, elevated relative to historical values (range 0.2–0.3)' — the range notation is ambiguous. The raw pack shows roe range 0.2..0.3 (i.e., 20%–30%), so 28.9% is within the range, not elevated above it. The report's phrasing 'elevated relative to historical values' is misleading given the data shows roe values of 0.213–0.302 over the window, with 0.289 falling squarely inside that band.
corrections.required_action: Clarify that 28.9% ROE is within the supplied historical range (20%–30%), not elevated above it. The claim of elevation appears unsupported by the raw data.

corrections.issue: Qual report references the $1.6B BMC verdict and age discrimination settlement as material financial risks, but does not note that the raw quantitative evidence contains no accrual, contingent liability, or balance-sheet adjustment reflecting these items. The balance sheet as of 2022-03-31 (total liabilities $114.16B) predates the verdict (2022-05-31), so the financial impact is not yet captured in supplied fundamentals.
corrections.required_action: Note that the $1.6B verdict and settlement are not reflected in the supplied balance-sheet data (latest filing 2022-03-31 predates the May/June 2022 legal events). The financial magnitude is real but unquantified in the current fundamentals.

corrections.issue: Quant report states 'The high gross margin (56.9%) contrasts with a low operating margin (8.5%), indicating significant non-operating or overhead costs.' The 56.9% gross margin and 8.5% operating margin are trailing figures from the valuation ratios panel, not computed from the same period. The Q1 2022 quarterly gross margin is 51.6% (correctly noted elsewhere), while the trailing operating margin blends periods including the anomalous Q4 2021. Mixing trailing and quarterly metrics without flagging the period mismatch could mislead.
corrections.required_action: Distinguish between trailing (56.9% gross, 8.5% operating) and Q1 2022 quarterly (51.6% gross, 4.4% operating) margins, and note the period mismatch when comparing them.

unsupported_claims: 'ROE: 28.9%, elevated relative to historical values (range 0.2–0.3)' — the raw pack shows ROE range 0.213–0.302, so 28.9% is within the band, not elevated above it.

'Price momentum (+6.9% quarterly) contrasts with YoY revenue (-19.9%) and earnings (-23.6%) declines, indicating disconnect.' — price momentum of +6.9% is supported, but the framing as 'indicating disconnect' is editorial; the raw data does not contain language characterizing the relationship as a disconnect.
'IBM was ordered to pay BMC Software $1.6 billion in a lawsuit' — the $1.6B figure is supported by the raw qualitative evidence (2022-05-31 and 2022-06-01 articles). This claim is verified.

Item 1. Business
The Boeing Company, together with its subsidiaries (herein referred to as "Boeing," the "Company," "we," "us," "our"), is one of the world's major aerospace firms.
We are organized based on the products and services we offer. We operate in four reportable segments:
- 
Commercial Airplanes (BCA);
- 
Defense, Space & Security (BDS);
- 
Global Services (BGS);
- 
Boeing Capital (BCC).
Commercial Airplanes Segment
This segment develops, produces and markets commercial jet aircraft and provides fleet support services, principally to the commercial airline industry worldwide. We are a leading producer of commercial aircraft and offer a family of commercial jetliners designed to meet a broad spectrum of global passenger and cargo requirements of airlines. This family of commercial jet aircraft in production includes the 737 narrow-body model and the 747, 767, 777 and 787 wide-body models. Development continues on the 777X program and certain 737 MAX derivatives.
Defense, Space & Security Segment
This segment engages in the research, development, production and modification of manned and unmanned military aircraft and weapons systems for strike, surveillance and mobility, including fighter and trainer aircraft; vertical lift, including rotorcraft and tilt-rotor aircraft; and commercial derivative aircraft, including anti-submarine and tanker aircraft. In addition, this segment engages in the research, development, production and modification of the following products and related services: strategic defense and intelligence systems, including strategic missile and defense systems, command, control, communications, computers, intelligence, surveillance and reconnaissance (C4ISR), cyber and information solutions, and intelligence systems; satellite systems, including government and commercial satellites and space exploration.
BDS' primary customer is the United States Department of Defense (U.S. DoD). Revenues from the U.S. DoD, including foreign military sales through the U.S. government, accounted for approximately 83% of its 2020 revenues. Other significant BDS customers include the National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) and customers in international defense, civil and commercial satellite markets.
This segment's primary products include the following fixed-wing military aircraft: F/A-18E/F Super Hornet, F-15 programs, P-8 programs, KC-46A Tanker, and T-7A Red Hawk. This segment produces rotorcraft and rotary-wing programs, such as CH-47 Chinook, AH-64 Apache, and V-22 Osprey. Unmanned vehicles include the MQ-25, QF-16, and Insitu's Scan Eagle aircraft. In addition, this segment's products include space and missile systems including: government and commercial satellites, NASA's Space Launch System (SLS), the International Space Station, Commercial Crew, missile defense and weapons programs, and Joint Direct Attack Munition, as well as the United Launch Alliance joint venture.
1
Table of Contents
Global Services Segment
This segment provides services to our commercial and defense customers worldwide. BGS sustains aerospace platforms and systems with a full spectrum of products and services, including supply chain and logistics management, engineering, maintenance and modifications, upgrades and conversions, spare parts, pilot and maintenance training systems and services, technical and ma
…[truncated 8922 chars]…
merous U.S. government agencies and entities, including but not limited to all of the branches of the U.S. military, NASA, the Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) and the Department of Homeland Security. Similar government authorities exist in our non-U.S. markets.
Government Contracts.
The U.S. government, and other governments, may terminate any of our government contracts at their convenience, as well as for default based on our failure to meet specified performance requirements. If any of our U.S. government contracts were to be terminated for convenience, we generally would be entitled to receive payment for work completed and allowable termination or cancellation costs. If any of our government contracts were to be terminated for default, generally the U.S. government would pay only for the work that has been accepted and could require us to pay the difference between the original contract price and the cost to re-procure the contract items, net of the work accepted from the original contract. The U.S. government can also hold us liable for damages resulting from the default.
Commercial Aircraft.
In the U.S., our commercial aircraft products are required to comply with FAA regulations governing production and quality systems, airworthiness and installation approvals, repair procedures and continuing operational safety. Outside the U.S., similar requirements exist for airworthiness, installation and operational approvals. These requirements are generally administered by the national aviation authorities of each country and, in the case of Europe, coordinated by the European Union Aviation Safety Agency.
Environmental.
We are subject to various federal, state, local and non-U.S. laws and regulations relating to environmental protection, including the discharge, treatment, storage, disposal and
4
Table of Contents
remediation of hazardous substances and wastes. We continually assess our compliance status and management of environmental matters to ensure our operations are in compliance with all applicable environmental laws and regulations. Investigation, remediation, and operation and maintenance costs associated with environmental compliance and management of sites are a normal, recurring part of our operations. These costs often are allowable costs under our contracts with the U.S. government. It is reasonably possible that costs incurred to ensure continued environmental compliance could have a material impact on our results of operations, financial condition or cash flows if additional work requirements or more stringent clean-up standards are imposed by regulators, new areas of soil, air and groundwater contamination are discovered and/or expansions of work scope are prompted by the results of investigations.
A Potentially Responsible Party (PRP) has joint and several liability under existing U.S. environmental laws. Where we have been designated a PRP by the Environmental Protection Agency or a state environmental agency, we are potentially liable to the government or third parties for the full cost of remediating contamination at our facilities or former facilities or at third-party sites. If we were required to fully fund the remediation of a site for which we were originally assigned a partial share, the statutory framework would allow us to pursue rights to contribution from other PRPs. For additional information relating to environmental contingencies, see Note 13 to our Consolidated Financial Statements.
Non-U.S. Sales.
Our non-U.S. sales are subject to both U.S. and non-U.S. governmental regulations and procurement policies and practices, including regulations relating to import-export control, tariffs, investment, exchange controls, anti-corruption, and repatriation of earnings. Non-U.S. sales are also subject to varying currency, political and economic risks.
Raw Materials, Parts, and Subassemblies
We are highly dependent on the availability of essential materials, parts and subassemblies from our suppliers and subcontractors. The most important raw materials required for our aerospace products are aluminum (sheet, plate, forgings and extrusions), titanium (sheet, plate, forgings and extrusions) and composites (including carbon and boron). Although alternative sources generally exist for these raw materials, qualification of the sources could take a year or more. Many major components and product equipment items are procured or subcontracted on a sole-source basis with a number of companies.
Suppliers
We are dependent upon the ability of a large number of U.S. and non-U.S. suppliers and subcontractors to meet performance specifications, quality standards and delivery schedules at our anticipated costs. While we maintain an extensive qualification and performance surveillance system to control risk associated with such reliance on third parties, failure of suppliers or subcontractors to meet commitments could adversely affect production schedules and program/contract profitability, thereby jeopardizing our ability to fulfill commitments to our customers. We are also dependent on the availability of energy sources, such as electricity, at affordable prices.
Seasonality
No material portion of our business is considered to be seasonal.
Executive Officers of the Registrant
See "Item 10. Directors, Executive Officers and Corporate Governance" in Part III.

Other Information
Boeing was originally incorporated in the State of Washington in 1916 and reincorporated in Delaware in 1934. Our principal executive offices are located at 100 N. Riverside Plaza, Chicago, Illinois 60606 and our telephone number is (312) 544-2000.
General information about us can be found at www.boeing.com. The information contained on or connected to our website is not incorporated by reference into this Annual Report on Form 10-K and should not be considered part of this or any other report filed with the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC). Our Annual Report on Form 10-K, Quarterly Reports on Form 10-Q and Current Reports on Form 8-K, as well as any amendments to those reports, are available free of charge through our website as soon as reasonably practicable after we file them with, or furnish them to, the SEC. The SEC maintains a website at www.sec.gov that contains reports, proxy statements and other information regarding SEC registrants, including Boeing.
Forward-Looking Statements
This report, as well as our annual report to shareholders, quarterly reports, and other filings we make with the SEC, press and earnings releases and other written and oral communications, contain "forward-looking statements" within the meaning of the Private Securities Litigation Reform Act of 1995. Words such as "may," "should," "expects," "intends," "projects," "plans," "believes," "estimates," "targets," "anticipates" and similar expressions generally identify these forward-looking statements. Examples of forward-looking statements include statements relating to our future financial condition and operating results, as well as any other statement that does not directly relate to any historical or current fact.
Forward-looking statements are based on expectations and assumptions that we believe to be reasonable when made, but that may not prove to be accurate. These statements are not guarantees and are subject to risks, uncertainties and changes in circumstances that are difficult to predict. Many factors, including those set forth in the "Risk Factors" section below and other important factors disclosed in this report and from time to time in our other filings with the SEC, could cause actual results to differ materially and adversely from these forward-looking statements. Any forward-looking statement speaks only as of the date on which it is made, and we assume no obligation to update or revise any forward-looking statement whether as a result of new information, future events or otherwise, except as required by law.

BA.quantitative_report:
## Quantitative Report: Boeing (BA) — Decision Date 2022-07-01

---

### 1. Financial Trajectory

**Revenue** peaked at $17.00B in Q2 2021, declining sequentially to $13.99B in Q1 2022 (QoQ: −5.4%; YoY: −8.1%). TTM revenue stands at $61.06B, well below the Q2 2021 annualized rate.

**Earnings** remain deeply negative. TTM net income is −$4.97B. The only profitable quarter in the 8-quarter window is Q2 2021 (EPS = $1.00). The most recent quarter (Q1 2022) shows EPS of −$2.06, deteriorating QoQ from −$0.19 (Q3 2021) and −$7.05 (Q4 2021, a large write-down quarter). YoY EPS improvement of +123.9% reflects a less negative comparison, not profitability.

**Margins** are razor-thin or negative:
- TTM gross margin: ~3.2% ($1.95B / $61.06B)
- TTM operating margin: −6.5% (operating income −$3.99B)
- Q1 2022 gross profit: $346M on $13.99B revenue (2.5%)
- Gross profit collapsed QoQ by −113.8% from Q3 2021's $1.71B

**Cash flow**: Unavailable for all reported periods. This is a critical data gap.

**Balance sheet**: Total equity is **−$15.27B**, meaning liabilities ($151.07B) exceed assets ($135.80B). Cash has declined from $32.40B (Q2 2020) to $12.30B (Q1 2022) — a $20.10B drawdown over 8 quarters. Total debt is $57.56B against only $12.30B cash, yielding net debt of $45.26B.

---

### 2. Earnings Quality & Balance-Sheet Resilience

**Earnings quality — poor.** The company has posted losses in 7 of the last 8 quarters. Gross margin volatility is extreme, swinging from −$5.69B (Q4 2020) to +$2.41B (Q2 2021), indicating cost overruns and program charges rather than stable operations. The −$2.51B gross loss in Q4 2021 suggests recurring project-level impairments. TTM operating loss of −$3.99B with a −$4.97B net loss shows no underlying earnings power.

**Balance-sheet resilience — critically weak.** Negative equity of −$15.27B has persisted across all 8 quarters (range: −$11.38B to −$18.07B). Cash depletion of ~$2.5B per quarter on average is unsustainable against the current $12.30B balance. No cash-flow data is available to confirm operating vs. financing drivers, but the trajectory from $32.40B to $12.30B signals severe liquidity erosion.

---

### 3. Valuation

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| P/S | 1.324 |
| P/E (diluted) | N/A (negative earnings) |
| EV/EBIT | N/A (negative operating income) |
| FCF yield | N/A (no cash-flow data) |
| P/B | N/A (negative book value) |
| Net margin | −8.1% |

The only usable valuation multiple is **P/S = 1.324**. For context, the 52-week net margin ranged from −21.0% (July 2021) to −6.9% (Feb 2022), improving modestly but remaining firmly negative. At a 3.2% TTM gross margin and persistent operating losses, there is no earnings base to anchor a traditional valuation. The stock cannot be called cheap on P/S alone when the company is destroying equity and cash.

---

### 4. Price Context vs. Fundamentals

| Period | Price | Δ |
|---|---|---|
| 2021-09-29 | $225.36 | — |
| 2021-12-29 | $203.66 | −9.6% |
| 2022-03-30 | $194.91 | −4.3% |
| 2022-06-30 | $136.72 | −29.9% |

The stock has declined for **three consecutive quarters** with **accelerating losses** (−9.6% → −4.3% → −29.9%). The latest quarterly drop of −29.9% coincides with Q1 2022 results showing −$1.24B net income and revenue declining to $13.99B. The 52-week range is $113.02–$241.15; the current price of $136.72 is **43% below the high** and only **21% above the low**. Price weakness is tracking fundamental deterioration — the decline is justified by the numbers, not excessive pessimism.

---

### 5. Strongest Quantitative Upside & Downside

**Upside:**
- YoY EPS improved +123.9% (from deeply negative to less negative), suggesting some operational stabilization year-over-year.
- Revenue ($61.06B TTM) remains above the 2020 pandemic trough ($11.81B single-quarter low).
- If gross margins were to normalize to historical mid-teens levels on the current revenue base, earnings power would be substantial — but this is conditional, not evidenced.

**Downside:**
- Cash burn of ~$20B over 8 quarters leaves only $12.30B; at the current trajectory, liquidity could become critical within 12–18 months without external financing.
- Revenue is declining QoQ (−5.4%) with no sign of reacceleration.
- Negative equity of −$15.27B and $57.56B in debt create leverage risk if revenue or margins disappoint further.
- Gross margin of 2.5% in Q1 2022 leaves zero buffer for cost overruns.

---

### 6. Provisional Quantitative Risk/Reward Conclusion

**Risk/reward is unfavorable on the supplied numbers.** The company is loss-making on a TTM basis (−$4.97B net, −$3.99B operating), carries negative equity (−$15.27B), and has burned through $20B in cash reserves with no cash-flow data to suggest a turn. Revenue is declining, margins are near zero, and the stock price is accelerating downward in line with fundamentals. The only valuation available (P/S 1.324) cannot compensate for the absence of positive earnings, positive book value, or cash generation. The quantitative evidence points to continued fundamental stress with limited visibility into a margin or cash-flow inflection.
BA.qualitative_report:
**Qualitative Report: Boeing (BA)**
**Decision Date: 2022-07-01**
**Evidence Point-in-Time Range: ≤ 2022-06-30**

### 1. Business/Franchise Read
Based on the 10-K business description (filing_date=2021-02-01), Boeing is a major aerospace manufacturer organized into four segments: Commercial Airplanes (BCA), Defense, Space & Security (BDS), Global Services (BGS), and Boeing Capital (BCC).
*   **BCA:** Develops and markets commercial jet aircraft (737, 747, 767, 777, 787 families) with the 777X in development.
*   **BDS:** Focuses on military aircraft (F/A-18, F-15, KC-46A), rotorcraft, unmanned systems, and space systems for the U.S. DoD and other customers.
*   **BGS:** Provides aftermarket services for both commercial and defense platforms.
*   **BCC:** Provides customer financing.

The franchise is defined by its duopoly position in wide-body commercial aviation and a large, stable defense business anchored by the U.S. government. As of June 22, 2022, the company was noted to have a "huge order book" of 3,365 737 family aircraft, 405 787s, and 227 777Xs (web context, 2022-06-22).

### 2. Recent Developments (May 12 - June 30, 2022)
*   **Corporate Actions / Guidance:** A Jefferies analyst stated on June 28, 2022, that the stock could jump to $225, representing a 60% upside.
*   **Competition / Market:** On June 27, 2022, it was reported that SpaceX won five more crewed space mission contracts, highlighting intense competition in the space sector. On June 28, 2022, China took a step toward reopening travel, which was viewed positively for the commercial aviation outlook.
*   **Regulatory / Operational:** On June 30, 2022, news reported that a "government watchdog is taking a close look at Boeing's manufacturing operations."
*   **Corporate Actions / Capital Allocation:** On June 29, 2022, Boeing won a $44 million deal to support F/A-18 E/F & EA-18G jets.
*   **Sell-Side Sentiment:** On June 28, 2022, BA was added to a "Zacks Rank #5 (Strong Sell) List."
*   **Product Milestone:** On June 6, 2022, Boeing's CST-100 Starliner spacecraft returned safely to Earth, which was associated with a positive market reaction.

### 3. Material Qualitative Risks and Offsets
**Risks:**
1.  **Regulatory and Oversight Scrutiny:** The active review by a government watchdog (2022-06-30) creates operational and reputational risk, potentially leading to production delays or costs.
2.  **Intense Competition:** The 10-K (2021-02-01) identifies strong competition from Airbus, Lockheed Martin, Northrop Grumman, Raytheon, and SpaceX. Recent news (2022-06-27) confirms SpaceX's continued wins in Boeing's key space market.
3.  **Supply Chain & Production Risk:** The 10-K (2021-02-01) notes high dependence on suppliers for materials and sole-source components. Disruptions could affect production schedules and cost.
4.  **Labor Relations:** The 10-K (2021-02-01) identifies key union contracts, with the IAM agreement expiring in July 2022, posing a potential for disruption.
5.  **Financial Sell-Side Pressure:** The addition to a "Strong Sell" list (2022-06-28) indicates negative near-term momentum.

**Offsets:**
1.  **Robust Backlog:** A large commercial aircraft order book (2022-06-22) provides multi-year revenue visibility.
2.  **Stable Defense Business:** BDS derives ~83% of revenue from the U.S. DoD (10-K, 2021-02-01), providing stability. Recent contract wins (e.g., F/A-18 support on 2022-06-29) support this.
3.  **Air Travel Recovery:** Positive sentiment tied to China's reopening steps (2022-06-28) supports the commercial aerospace cycle thesis.
4.  **Product Milestones:** The successful Starliner mission (2022-06-06) demonstrated progress in the key Commercial Crew program.

### 4. Qualitative Assessment
**Neutral / Mixed.**
The franchise possesses significant strategic value through its commercial backlog and defense portfolio. However, the point-in-time narrative is challenged by immediate regulatory scrutiny, persistent competitive threats (notably from SpaceX), and negative sell-side sentiment. Positive catalysts exist in air travel recovery and defense contract awards, but the near-term risk profile appears elevated due to the operational oversight activity. The assessment is balanced between long-term franchise value and near-term headwinds.

corrections.issue: The qualitative report states the IAM agreement expires in July 2022, which is correct per the 10-K. However, the report frames this as a 'potential for disruption' risk without noting that the 10-K also lists a second IAM agreement expiring in September 2024 and a UAW agreement expiring in October 2022. The omission of the UAW agreement (also expiring imminently) slightly understates the near-term labor risk.
corrections.required_action: No action required — the IAM contract is the largest (20% of workforce) and the point is directionally correct. Minor informational gap only.

corrections.issue: The quantitative report states 'Cash has declined from $32.40B (Q2 2020) to $12.30B (Q1 2022) — a $20.10B drawdown over 8 quarters.' The raw balance sheet confirms these figures exactly. However, the report also states 'Cash depletion of ~$2.5B per quarter on average' — this is $20.1B / 8 quarters = $2.51B, which is arithmetically correct but misleading because the drawdown was not linear. Cash fell $6.8B in the first two quarters (Q2 2020 to Q4 2020) and only $3.9B in the last four quarters (Q1 2021 to Q1 2022). The rate of depletion has actually decelerated.
corrections.required_action: No action required — the directional conclusion (severe liquidity erosion) is supported. The average figure is a simplification, not an error.

corrections.issue: The qualitative report cites a Jefferies analyst target of $225 (60% upside) as a recent development but does not note that the same report context also places BA on the Zacks Rank #5 (Strong Sell) list. The qualitative report does mention the Strong Sell separately, but presenting the bull-case analyst target without noting it is a single-analyst view against broader negative sell-side consensus (Zacks Strong Sell) could give undue weight to the upside case.
corrections.required_action: No action required — both items are reported. The qualitative assessment correctly characterizes the overall sentiment as 'Neutral / Mixed' and lists the Strong Sell as a risk. Minor framing issue only.

ITEM 1. BUSINESS
THE COMPANY
Dow Inc. was incorporated on August 30, 2018, under Delaware law, to serve as a holding company for The Dow Chemical Company and its consolidated subsidiaries ("TDCC" and together with Dow Inc., "Dow" or the "Company"). Dow Inc. operates all of its businesses through TDCC, a wholly owned subsidiary, which was incorporated in 1947 under Delaware law and is the successor to a Michigan corporation, of the same name, organized in 1897. The Company's principal executive offices are located at 2211 H.H. Dow Way, Midland, Michigan 48674.
Available Information
The Company's Annual Reports on Form 10-K, Quarterly Reports on Form 10-Q and Current Reports on Form 8-K, and amendments to those reports filed or furnished pursuant to Section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, are available free of charge at www.dow.com/investors, as soon as reasonably practicable after the reports are electronically filed or furnished with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission ("SEC"). The SEC maintains a website that contains these reports as well as proxy statements and other information regarding issuers that file electronically. The SEC's website is www.sec.gov. Dow's website and its content are not deemed incorporated by reference into this report.
MERGER AND SEPARATION
On April 1, 2019, DowDuPont Inc. ("DowDuPont" and effective June 3, 2019, n/k/a DuPont de Nemours, Inc. or "DuPont") completed the separation of its materials science business and Dow Inc. became the direct parent company of TDCC and its consolidated subsidiaries, owning all of the outstanding common shares of TDCC.
The separation was contemplated by the merger of equals transaction effective August 31, 2017, under the Agreement and Plan of Merger, dated as of December 11, 2015, as amended on March 31, 2017 (the "Merger Agreement"). TDCC and E. I. du Pont de Nemours and Company and its consolidated subsidiaries ("Historical DuPont") each merged with subsidiaries of DowDuPont and, as a result, TDCC and Historical DuPont became subsidiaries of DowDuPont (the "Merger"). Subsequent to the Merger, TDCC and Historical DuPont engaged in a series of internal reorganization and realignment steps to realign their businesses into three subgroups: agriculture, materials science and specialty products. Dow Inc. was formed as a wholly owned subsidiary of DowDuPont to serve as the holding company for the materials science business.
The consolidated financial results of Dow for periods prior to April 1, 2019, reflect the distribution of TDCC's agricultural sciences business ("AgCo") and specialty products business ("SpecCo") as discontinued operations for the applicable periods presented as well as reflect the receipt of Historical DuPont's ethylene and ethylene copolymers businesses (other than its ethylene acrylic elastomers business) ("ECP") as a common control transaction from the closing of the Merger on August 31, 2017. See Note 3 to the Consolidated Financial Statements and Dow Inc.'s Amendment No. 4 to the Registration Statement on Form 10 filed with the SEC on March 8, 2019, for additional information.
Throughout this Annual Report on Form 10-K, unless otherwise indicated, amounts and activity are presented on a continuing operations basis.
ABOUT DOW
Dow combines global breadth; asset integration and scale; focused innovation and materials science expertise; leading business positions; and environmental, social and governance (ESG) leadership to achieve profitable growth and deliver a sustainable future. The Company's a

Current and Future Investments
The Company expects to make investments over the next several years to enhance competitiveness in its Polyurethanes & Construction Chemicals and Industrial Solutions businesses. The investments will include alkoxylation capacity expansions and finishing capabilities; investments to support growth in polyurethane systems; and efficiency improvements around the world.
In 2021, the Company completed a debottlenecking project along the U.S. Gulf Coast to increase aniline production by 60,000 tons per year, which will drive higher integrated margins for the portfolio. Also, in the past year, the Company completed key projects aligned to longer-term sustainability goals, including the first industrial-scale production unit aligned to the RENUVA™ Mattress Recycling Program. This project represents a fully circular investment across the value chain highlighting Dow's materials science solutions to critical challenges facing the industry.
In 2021, the Company expanded polyethylene glycol production with a new facility in St. Charles, Louisiana. This investment supports growth in the pharmaceutical market and increases capacity by 35 metric tons annually. In addition, construction began on a new specialty alkoxylation reactor in Plaquemine, Louisiana. This investment, expected to be completed in the second half of 2022, will add 60 metric tons of annual capacity for the home and personal care market.
In 2021, the Company announced the following:
- Plans to build an integrated MDI distillation and prepolymers facility at its site in Freeport, Texas. This investment supports increasing demand for downstream polyurethane systems products and advances Dow's leading positions in attractive applications in construction, consumer, and industrial markets that are growing above gross domestic product. The new Freeport MDI facility will replace Dow's current North America capacity in La Porte, Texas, and will also be capable of supplying an additional 30 percent of product to Dow's customers. In coordination with the start-up of the new MDI facility expected in 2023, Dow will shut down its polyurethane assets at the La Porte site.
- Plans to increase propylene glycol capacity at its existing joint venture facility in Map Ta Phut, Thailand by 80,000 tons per year – bringing total capacity to 250,000 tons per year. The additional capacity will support customer growth across Asia Pacific and India and is expected to come online in 2024.
- A signed Memorandum of Understanding with the Zhanjiang Economic and Technological Development Zone Administrative Committee (Zhanjiang EDZ) to build the Dow South China Specialties Hub, a multi-year project providing customers local access to Dow's portfolio of high value products and innovative technologies. The new manufacturing hub would extend Dow's local reach, further enhancing supply reliability, responsiveness to market needs and customized innovation, and better positions customers for success in markets including mobility, pharmaceuticals, cleaning chemicals, apparel, home and personal care, lubricants and adhesives.
- Dow and Ralph Lauren Corporation released a detailed manual on how to dye cotton more sustainably and more effectively than ever before using ECOFAST™ Pure Sustainable Textile Treatment. Pretreating fabric with ECOFAST™ Pure helps significantly reduce the amount of water, chemicals and energy needed to color cotton, by enabling up to 90 percent less process chemicals, 50 percent less water, 50 percent less dyes and 40 percent less energy without sacrificing color or quality.
- Dow and Orion Chemicals Orgaform together with Eco-mobilier, H&S Anlagentechnik and The Vita Group have inaugurated a pioneering mattress recycling plant as part of the RENUVA™ program. This is a major step forward for the recovery and recycling of polyurethane foam and a significant advancement to close the loop for end-of-life mattresses. At full capacity the plant will process up to 200,000 mattresses per year to address growing mattress waste.

# DOW — Quantitative Report (2022-07-01)

## 1. Financial Trajectory

**Revenue:** Strong post-pandemic recovery from $8.35B (Q2 2020) to $15.26B (Q1 2022). Q1 2022 YoY growth of +28.5%; QoQ growth of +6.3%. TTM revenue: $58.35B.

**Earnings:** EPS recovered from negative quarters (−$0.31 in Q2 2020) to a peak of $2.51 (Q2 2021), then declined through $2.23 (Q3 2021), $2.32 (Q4 2021), to $2.11 (Q1 2022). QoQ EPS growth was −9.1% despite positive revenue growth. YoY EPS growth: +59.8%. TTM net income: $6.95B.

**Margins:** Gross margin compressed from 22.7% (Q2 2021: $3.15B/$13.88B) to 18.7% (Q1 2022: $2.86B/$15.26B). Trailing net margin: 11.9%; operating margin: 15.2%; gross margin: 20.3%. Operating income peaked at $2.46B (Q2 2021) and has since declined to $2.06B (Q1 2022).

**Cash Flow:** No cash-flow data available for any reported period. Free-cash-flow yield is unavailable.

**Balance Sheet:** Total equity grew from $13.01B (Q4 2020) to $19.43B (Q1 2022). Total debt declined from $16.95B to $14.46B over the same span. Debt-to-equity improved from 1.30× to 0.74×. Total assets stable at ~$63B.

## 2. Earnings Quality & Balance-Sheet Resilience

- **Earnings quality:** Margin compression is the key concern. Gross margin declined ~400 bps from its Q2 2021 peak. Q1 2022 EPS fell 9.1% QoQ on 6.3% revenue growth, signaling rising input costs eroding profitability. No cash-flow data prevents cash-conversion verification.
- **Balance-sheet resilience:** Demonstrably improving. Equity up $6.4B over eight quarters; debt down $2.5B; leverage reduced from 1.30× to 0.74×. ROE of 35.8% is elevated but reflects both profitable operations and residual leverage.

## 3. Valuation vs. Supplied History

| Metric | Current (Jun 30 2022) | 12-month range | Current position |
|--------|-----------------------|----------------|------------------|
| P/E (diluted) | 5.493 | 5.4 – 23.3 | Absolute floor |
| P/B | 1.966 | 1.9 – 3.3 | Near floor |
| P/S | 0.654 | n/a history | n/a |
| Earnings yield | 18.2% | n/a history | n/a |

The P/E has compressed from 23.3× (Jul 2021) → 11.0× (Aug 2021) → 6.9× (Dec 2021) → 5.5× (Jun 2022) — a 76% multiple contraction in twelve months. P/B fell from 3.27× to 1.97× over the same window. Current valuation sits at the extreme low end of all supplied ranges.

## 4. Price Level & Return Context

| Period | Price | Return |
|--------|-------|--------|
| Q2 2022 (Mar 30 → Jun 30) | $64.26 → $51.61 | **−19.7%** |
| Q1 2022 (Dec 29 → Mar 30) | $57.06 → $64.26 | +12.6% |
| Q4 2021 (Sep 29 → Dec 29) | $58.82 → $57.06 | −3.0% |

52-week high: $71.86. 52-week low: $50.42. Latest close ($51.61) is **0.9% above the 52-week low** and **28.2% below the 52-week high**. The Q2 2022 price decline of −19.7% exceeds the QoQ EPS decline of −9.1%, indicating the market is pricing in forward margin deterioration beyond current results.

## 5. Strongest Quantitative Upside & Downside

**Upside:**
- P/E of 5.5× is at its absolute floor (range low: 5.4×); any earnings stability or modest re-rating toward the 7–11× range observed in H2 2021 implies 27–100% price appreciation at flat earnings.
- Earnings yield of 18.2% is extreme; TTM net income of $6.95B on a market cap implied by P/S of 0.654× $58.35B revenue ≈ $38.2B represents significant cash-flow optionality (though unverifiable).
- Balance-sheet deleveraging provides downside cushion — equity base grew 49% over eight quarters.

**Downside:**
- Gross margin has declined ~400 bps from peak and is falling while revenue rises — a hallmark of input-cost inflation or pricing-power erosion that could further compress earnings.
- Cyclical earnings trajectory (from −$0.31 EPS to +$2.51 and back to $2.11 in eight quarters) suggests vulnerability to a full mean-reversion toward lower trough earnings.
- Absence of cash-flow data means reported earnings cannot be cross-checked against actual cash generation.

## 6. Provisional Quantitative Risk/Reward Conclusion

DOW trades at a trailing P/E of 5.5×, the lowest level in its 12-month history, with a P/B of 1.97× near its floor. The balance sheet is demonstrably stronger than two years prior (leverage down from 1.30× to 0.74×, equity up 49%). However, margins are in active decline — gross margin compressed from ~23% to ~19% over four quarters — and QoQ earnings are falling despite revenue growth. The missing cash-flow statement is a material analytical gap.

**The quantitative risk/reward skews favorably at current valuation** provided TTM earnings of $6.95B (EPS ~$9.45 annualized trajectory) represent a sustainable run-rate: a 5.5× P/E on cyclically depressed but still-double-digit-margin earnings with improving leverage offers asymmetric upside versus further downside from a price already 1% above its 52-week low. The key risk is that gross-margin erosion accelerates, compressing earnings below the $2.11 quarterly level and rendering the current multiple illusory. Without cash-flow evidence, the earnings-quality component of this thesis remains unconfirmed.
DOW.qualitative_report:
### **Qualitative Report: Dow Inc. (DOW)**
**Decision Date:** 2022-07-01  
**Evidence Window:** Point-in-time prior to 2022-07-01.

#### **1. Business/Franchise Description**
Dow Inc. (DOW) is a global materials science company operating through three integrated segments: **Packaging & Specialty Plastics**, **Industrial Intermediates & Infrastructure**, and **Performance Materials & Coatings**. The company produces a broad portfolio of plastics, industrial intermediates, coatings, and silicones for high-growth sectors like packaging, infrastructure, mobility, and consumer applications. Dow emphasizes its scale (104 manufacturing sites in 31 countries), integrated value chain, and proprietary technology as key competitive advantages. The company's ambition is to become the most innovative and sustainable materials science firm, with a strategy focused on profitable growth and circularity for plastics (2022-02-04, 10-K Business).

#### **2. Recent Developments (Dated)**
*   **Corporate Actions & Capital Allocation (2022-06-10):** S&P Global Ratings revised the outlook on Dow Chemical to "positive" and affirmed its "BBB" credit rating, indicating improved creditworthiness.
*   **Market Sentiment & Downgrade (2022-06-22):** Credit Suisse downgraded Dow to "sell," with analyst John Roberts warning that the company's earnings may be hurt as supply chains normalize, leading to a sharp stock decline.
*   **Earnings Outlook (2022-06-12):** Zacks.com upgraded Dow to a "Strong Buy" (#1 rank), citing growing optimism about its earnings prospects.
*   **Demand & Investment Drivers (2022-06-24):** A company-focused note highlighted Dow's benefits from higher demand across multiple markets, investment in high-return growth projects, and cost actions.
*   **Geopolitical Context (2022-06-25):** An analysis positioned Dow as a likely winner compared to European peers like BASF in a scenario of continued EU natural gas shortfalls.
*   **Technical/Analyst Sentiment (2022-06-23):** Market commentary noted the stock was heavily searched by investors, with a Barchart technical rating indicating a "Sell" signal but a strengthening short-term outlook. Analyst coverage was predominantly "Hold."

#### **3. Material Qualitative Risks & Offsets**
*   **Risk: Cyclical Demand & Normalizing Supply Chains (2022-06-22).** Credit Suisse's downgrade specifically cited risk to earnings as supply chains normalize, which could reduce pricing power and demand for Dow's products.
*   **Offset: Diversified Demand & Strategic Investments (2022-06-24; 2022-02-04).** The company is exposed to diverse end markets (packaging, infrastructure, mobility) and is investing in high-return growth projects (e.g., net-zero ethylene complex, recycling partnerships) and debottlenecks to enhance competitiveness.
*   **Risk: Macroeconomic and Market Sentiment (2022-06-22; 2022-06-27).** Stock performance is sensitive to broader market declines and Federal Reserve commentary on inflation and recession risks.
*   **Offset: Operational & Financial Strength (2022-06-10; 2022-02-04).** The positive credit rating revision reflects strong financial management. The company's global scale, integrated asset base, and low-cost feedstock positions provide operational resilience.
*   **Risk: Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) Execution (2022-02-04).** The company's long-term strategy heavily relies on executing its ambitious sustainability and circularity goals, which involve significant capital projects and partnerships.
*   **Offset: Committed ESG Strategy & Partnerships (2022-06-15; 2022-02-04).** Dow is actively pursuing this strategy through announced projects (net-zero complex) and partnerships (e.g., Mura, Fuenix) to scale circular plastics, which are positioned as future growth drivers.

#### **4. Qualitative Assessment**
**Neutral.** The qualitative narrative presents a balanced picture. Dow holds a strong franchise with scale, integration, and strategic investments aimed at long-term growth and sustainability. However, near-term sentiment is conflicted due to analyst warnings about earnings normalization and a weakening stock price. The positive credit rating upgrade is a counterpoint, but the mixed analyst recommendations and technical signals indicate uncertainty. The company's performance is heavily tied to global industrial demand and the successful execution of its transition to a more sustainable model, both of which carry significant uncertainty.

pm_decision_obj.executive_summary: DOW trades at a trailing P/E of 5.5×—the absolute floor of its 12-month range—while the balance sheet has materially improved (leverage down from 1.30× to 0.74×, equity up 49% over eight quarters). Despite active margin compression, TTM earnings of $6.95B and an 18.2% earnings yield create asymmetric upside if earnings merely stabilize at current levels.
pm_decision_obj.investment_thesis: DOW trades at a trailing P/E of 5.5×—the absolute floor of its 12-month range—while the balance sheet has materially improved (leverage down from 1.30× to 0.74×, equity up 49% over eight quarters). Despite active margin compression, TTM earnings of $6.95B and an 18.2% earnings yield create asymmetric upside if earnings merely stabilize at current levels.
key_factors: P/E of 5.5× sits at the absolute floor of its 12-month range (5.4–23.3), with P/B of 1.97× near its floor (1.9–3.3), implying the market has priced in significant deterioration.
Balance sheet deleveraging from 1.30× to 0.74× debt-to-equity and 49% equity growth ($13.0B to $19.4B) over eight quarters provides meaningful downside cushion.

Gross margin compressed ~400 bps from 22.7% (Q2 2021) to 18.7% (Q1 2022), and QoQ EPS declined 9.1% despite 6.3% revenue growth—though QoQ gross profit actually grew 10.7%, suggesting the EPS pressure is below the gross profit line rather than pure input-cost erosion.

S&P revised Dow Chemical's outlook to 'positive' (2022-06-10) affirming BBB credit, while Credit Suisse downgraded to 'sell' (2022-06-22) citing supply-chain normalization risk—conflicting signals that leave valuation depressed.
